title: Will Russia's answer to the Falcon 9 rocket ever take flight?
gist: Roscosmos's Amur-LNG reusable rocket, once slated to debut in 2026, is now tracking toward 2031 flight tests of just a first-stage demonstrator per Deputy Director General Dmitry Baranov, leaving Russia far behind the US, China, and Japan in the reusable-rocket race SpaceX pioneered with Falcon 9.

insights.claim: Roscosmos's Amur-LNG reusable rocket, announced in 2020 with a promised 2026 debut, has effectively slipped to a 2031 flight-test target based on a placard shown at a May 2026 security forum - a five-year delay revealed over six years of development.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: Treat Roscosmos-announced timelines as unreliable; the program has already slipped roughly one year of schedule for every year of elapsed development.
insights.quote: when the Amur rocket was announced in 2020, its flight date was scheduled for 2026. Some six years later, its projected launch date has moved five years into the future.

insights.claim: Russia's answer to the Falcon 9 will not arrive any time soon and is unlikely to fly until at least two decades after SpaceX's first successful orbital rocket landing, per the author's assessment.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: Sets a realistic multi-decade horizon (mid-2030s or later) before Russia can field genuine reusable-launch capability comparable to Falcon 9.
insights.quote: The only reasonable conclusion is that Russia's answer to the Falcon 9 rocket will not arrive any time soon, and it is unlikely to do so until at least two decades after SpaceX first landed an orbital rocket.

insights.claim: Roscosmos official Dmitry Baranov, Deputy Director General for Rocket Programs, disclosed in an RBC interview that current Amur work is focused only on building a low-altitude first-stage 'demonstrator,' not the orbital vehicle itself.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: Confirms the program is still at an early hopper-test stage, far from orbital reusable flight despite six years of development.

insights.claim: The Amur hopper demonstrator is planned to run two flight tests starting in 2028: a sub-1km hover-and-return test with the engine running throughout, followed by a more ambitious 10km test involving engine shutoff, restart, and landing on deployable legs, modeled on a SpaceX Grasshopper-style profile.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: Even the demonstrator phase alone spans multiple years (2028+), pushing any orbital-capable Amur vehicle well past 2031.
insights.locator: This hopper vehicle, likely reminiscent of SpaceX's Grasshopper test bed

insights.claim: The RD-0169A engine intended to power Amur is still only in the preliminary stages of test firings, according to Russian officials.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: Engine immaturity is an early-stage bottleneck consistent with the program's repeated schedule slips and a further risk to the 2028 hopper-test target.

insights.claim: Baranov said the Amur vehicle is meant to 'definitely and fairly quickly' replace the Soyuz 2 rocket, currently Russia's workhorse for crewed and cargo launches to the ISS, but offered no new target date for that replacement.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: Absent a firm replacement date, expect Russia to keep relying on the expendable Soyuz 2 for ISS crew/cargo access well into the 2030s.
insights.locator: Soyuz 2 is probably safe for now

insights.claim: Amur-LNG is designed as a direct response to SpaceX's Falcon 9, featuring a reusable first stage and methane-fueled engines, and targets delivering 10.5 metric tons to low-Earth orbit in reusable mode.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: Defines the payload class Russia is aiming for (roughly Falcon 9's reusable-mode capacity) once and if the vehicle materializes.
insights.locator: Clearly designed in response to SpaceX's Falcon 9 rocket

insights.claim: China joined the orbital-launch-and-landing club the week before this article's publication, which the author frames as just the start of an aggressive public- and private-sector push into rocket reuse in China.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: China is now credibly ahead of Russia in demonstrated reusable-launch capability, reshaping the competitive order in the space-launch race.
insights.locator: Last week, China joined the club

insights.claim: Japan's space agency has been conducting reusable-rocket hop tests, and Honda has separately performed vertical reuse tests, showing reusable-launch development spreading beyond traditional space agencies to unconventional entrants like an automaker.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: Watch for new, non-traditional entrants (automakers, industrial conglomerates) entering reusable-launch R&D beyond dedicated space companies.
insights.locator: Also in Asia, Japan's space agency

insights.claim: In the US, SpaceX launches and lands reusable rockets every few days, Blue Origin has demonstrated landing and relaunching its large New Glenn booster despite it being temporarily grounded, and Stoke Space, Rocket Lab, and Relativity Space are all making credible progress toward partial or full reusability.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: The US reusable-launch bench is deep and multi-vendor, widening the capability gap versus Russia's single, delayed Amur effort.
insights.locator: In the United States, of course
